Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

- ACCURATE AND FAST DETECTION OF COPY NUM - - BER VARIATIONS FROM SHORT READ WHOLE GENOME SEQUENCING WITH DEEP CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORK

**Anonymous authors** Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

A copy number variant (CNV) is a type of genetic mutation where a stretch of DNA is lost or duplicated once or multiple times. CNVs play important roles in the development of diseases and complex traits. CNV detection with short-read DNA sequencing technology is challenging because CNVs significantly vary in size and are similar to DNA sequencing artifacts. Many methods have been developed but still yield unsatisfactory results with high computational costs. Here, we propose CNV-Net, a novel approach for CNV detection using a six-layer convolutional neural network. We encode DNA sequencing information into RGB images and train the convolutional neural network with these images. The fitted convolutional neural network can then be used to predict CNVs from DNA sequencing data. We benchmark CNV-Net with two high-quality whole-genome sequencing datasets available from the Genome in a Bottle Consortium, considered as gold standard benchmarking datasets for CNV detection. ## 1 INTRODUCTION

A copy number variant (CNV) is a genetic mutation where a stretch of DNA is completely lost or repeated more than once compared to a reference genome. CNV sizes range from 50 bases to 3 million bases or more with two major types: duplication if the DNA sequence is repeated and deletion if a DNA sequence is missing. CNVs are spread along a genome, which account for 4.8% to 9.5% of human genome (Zarrei et al., 2015). They are known to influence many complex diseases including autism (Sebat et al., 2007), bipolar disorder (Green et al., 2016), schizophrenia (Stefansson et al., 2008), and Alzheimer’ disease (Cuccaro et al., 2017) as well as gene expression (Chiang et al., 2017). As CNVs may overlap with a large portion of a genome, such as one gene or even multiple genes, their effect on disease may be substantially larger than that of single nucleotide variants, and hence CNVs often play important roles in the genetic mechanisms of diseases and complex traits.

With the advent of next-generation DNA sequencing technologies over the past decade, the resolution and scale of CNV detection has been greatly improved as large-scale sequencing studies become feasible. However, CNVs are still challenging to detect from short-read next-generation DNA sequencing techniques due to the significantly varying sizes of CNVs and their similarity with common DNA sequencing artifacts. Many computational methods have been developed for the discovery of CNVs from short-read DNA sequencing data, but their performance is often unsatisfactory due to low accuracy and high computational cost (Kosugi et al., 2019). The main reason for this is that previous methods (Rausch et al., 2012; Abyzov et al., 2011) mainly rely on the statistical analysis of the signals from read alignments (a process that maps reads from DNA sequencing data to the reference genome), which often fail to employ all useful features of DNA sequencing data and require significant computational resources. Therefore, there is a need for a novel, sophisticated computational tool to improve CNV detection with higher accuracy and efficiency.
Here, we present CNV-Net, a new approach to identify CNVs from DNA sequencing data using a six-layer deep convolutional neural network (CNN). CNV-Net first encodes the reads and their

related information from DNA sequencing data such as the type of base, base coverage (the number of reads covering a position), and base quality of each position into the RGB channels of images. Then it uses a deep CNN to predict these images as deletions, duplications, or false positives (not a CNV) breakpoints. Note that a CNV is defined by two breakpoints, that is, its start and end positions. Although it has been shown that CNNs such as DeepVariant (Poplin et al., 2018) and Clairvoyante (Luo et al., 2019) can accurately detect single nucleotide variants from similarly encoded images or tensors, few CNNs have been designed for the identification of CNVs. The key advantage of CNVNet over other methods is the application of deep learning, which allows learning features with higher complexity from DNA sequencing data. We train the network with the Genome in a Bottle Consortium NA12878 dataset (Pendleton et al., 2015), and demonstrate CNV-Net performance on a high-quality benchmarking dataset acquired from a well-studied cell line, HG002 (Zook et al., 2020). We also compare its performance to that of previous methods.

## 2 METHODS

CNV-Net first encodes sequencing information into pileup images and classifies each image as a deletion, duplication, or false positive (not a CNV). In this section, we describe how we generate pileup images, that is, the input to CNV-Net, as well as the design of the CNN.

## 2.1 PILEUP IMAGE GENERATION

We convert the information of mapped reads from DNA sequencing data (provided in BAM file format) and reference genome information to 201 _×_ 55 pixel image representation of 201-base wide regions. Training data pileup images contain a CNV breakpoint (start or end position) centered in the image, capturing the 100 positions left and right each CNV breakpoint. Using the RGB channels of an image, this snapshot is able to capture features of the reads at specific locations in the reference genome.

Figure 1: 201 _×_ 55 pixel pileup image. Feature rows are labeled by brackets and RGB channels are labeled by curly braces. Reference rows are encoded in R channel by base A: 250, G: 180, T: 100, C: 30, N: 0. Coverage rows are encoded in RG channels by the number of mapped reads covering each position. Reads are encoded in RGB channels by base types, base quality, and strand directionality positive: 70, negative: 240.

As shown in Figure 1, the first five rows of the image capture the reference genome across a region of 201 base pairs by encoding the R channel of each pixel with an individual base in that position: A’s as 250, G’s as 180, T’s as 100, C’s as 30, and N’s as 0. Under the five rows representing the reference genome are another five rows that capture the read depth (coverage) at that position. The R and G channels are encoded with an adjusted read depth value between 0 and 255. The following 45 rows capture the mapped reads from DNA sequencing. The R channel encodes for the nucleotide, which is the same as the reference genome, and the G channel encodes for the base quality found in the BAM file, which is adjusted to be between 0 and 255. Lastly, the B channel encodes for strand directionality: 70 if positive and 240 if negative.

Each 201 _×_ 55 pixel pileup image, thus, captures coverage and reads specific to the 201-base region of the reference genome such that the sequencing data may be re-created from a series of consecutive pileup images. If there are more than 45 reads mapping to a specific region, those reads are discarded; we instead compensate by capturing this information through the rows that encodes coverage. If no reads map to the region, the image is left blank below the first five rows for the reference genome.

## 2.2 DESIGN OF CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORK

Figure 2: CNV-Net architecture. CNV-Net employs a six-layer convolutional neural network for detecting copy number variations from DNA sequencing data. Descriptions under each layer indicate: type of layer, dimension of layer in parenthesis (input layer: height _×_ width _×_ channels, convolutional layer: height _×_ width _×_ filters, and fully connected layer: nodes), kernel size in brackets. The model predicts the probability of deletion (“DEL”), duplication (“DUP”), and false positive (“FP”) breakpoint presence in each input pileup image.

As presented in Figure 2, CNV-Net consists of four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers with a total of 5,110,979 parameters. Each of the four convolutional layers uses 3 _×_ 3 kernels and the RELU activation function. Each convolutional layer is followed by batch normalization, average pooling, and 0.4 dropout. Arrays are flattened in between the four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers followed by softmax output to predictions for CNV breakpoint presence in the 201-base window: duplication, deletion, or false positive (not a CNV).

In training CNV-net, we use a standard Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba, 2014) with a sparse categorical cross-entropy loss function. We use a cyclical learning rate initialized at 1e-5 and maximal 1e-2. To reduce overfitting, we train the model until the accuracy cannot be improved in 15 epochs. The 201-base regions are centered around the CNV breakpoints, that is, the starts and ends of CNVs in the training set capturing the 100 positions directly before and after each CNV breakpoint. False positive images include random 201-base regions outside of CNVs. From the NA12878 dataset, a total of 52,243 pileup images are labeled as duplications, deletions, and false positives from the training set randomly split into 70%, 20%, 10% categories for training set, validation set, and test set for the network, respectively.

## 3 RESULTS

In this study, we use two standard structural variation benchmarking datasets: NA12878 and HG002. Both these benchmarking datasets are mapped to the GRCh37 reference genome. NA12878 is a well-studied genome provided by the Genome in a Bottle Consortium. It is whole-genome sequenced with 50 _×_ coverage and each read in the sequencing data is 101 bases long. From the NA12878 image dataset (Pendleton et al., 2015), we generate images for 22,936 duplications, 18,614 deletions, and 10,693 false positives (not CNVs) breakpoints. The HG002 dataset (Zook et al., 2020) is another robust benchmarking dataset derived by the Genome in a Bottle Consortium, which is sequenced with 60 _×_ coverage. Reads in the HG002 dataset are all 148 bases long. As we only consider CNVs passing the quality control filter, this dataset contains 174 duplications, 10,668 deletions, and 5,205 false positives breakpoints post-filtering.

We train the convolutional neural network on the NA12878 dataset and test the fitted model on the HG002 dataset. We also compare CNV-Net with popular existing tools for CNV detection, including BreakDancer (Chen et al., 2009), CNVnator (Abyzov et al., 2011), Delly (Rausch et al.,

3

2012), Lumpy (Layer et al., 2014), and Manta (Chen et al., 2016). It is important to note that CNVNet makes predictions using the pileup images of CNVs where their start and end positions are given, and hence we assume that candidate CNV positions are given as input to CNV-Net. However, other methods find the candidate CNV positions using the information on how reads are mapped to the reference genome and detect CNVs on those candidate CNV positions.

The NA12878 dataset is preprocessed and converted into RGB images as described in the Methods section. We split the CNVs in NA12878 into 70%, 20%, 10% for training set, validation set, and test set, respectively. We train the CNV-Net with the training set and report its performance metrics on the test set. As shown in Table 1, CNV-Net identifies duplications with F1-score of 0.7833, deletions with F1-score of 0.7473, and false positives with F1-score of 0.7124. This result shows that CNV-Net achieves high accuracy in the test set of the NA12878 dataset.

Table 1: Performance of CNV-Net on the test set of the NA12878 dataset

|**CNV types**|**Precision**|**Recall**|**F1-score**|
|---|---|---|---|
|Duplications|0.8376|0.7356|0.7833|
|Deletions|0.7885|0.7102|0.7473|
|Falsepositives|0.6046|0.8672|0.7124|

Next, we evaluate our fitted CNV-Net on the HG002 dataset and compare it with five existing tools for CNV detection. This training scheme represents the real data analysis as we will train CNVNet with CNVs from known genomes and testing it on different genomes. As demonstrated in Table 2, CNV-Net achieves the highest F1-score in CNV detection in the HG002 dataset among all six methods. It also has the highest recall and the second highest precision. This result shows that CNV-Net is more accurate in CNV detection than current methods.

We then compare the time cost of applying different methods to the HG002 dataset. CNV-Net is significantly faster than other methods (Table 3). That is because a fitted convolutional neural network is efficient in making predictions, however, it may be time-consuming during model training. Run time is effectively front-loaded into model training instead of a concurrent statistical analysis. Another reason for this can be that CNV-Net makes predictions on candidate CNVs so it does not need to spend time searching for candidate CNVs in DNA sequencing data as if other methods do.

Table 3: Runtime of CNV-Net and other methods on the HG002 dataset

|**Tools**|**Runtime**|
|---|---|
|BreakDancer|92 minutes|
|CNVnator|51 minutes|
|Delly|565 minutes|
|Lumpy|46 minutes|
|Manta|36 minutes|
|**CNV-Net**|**16.62 seconds**|

## 4 CONCLUSION

CNV-Net encodes DNA sequencing information into RGB images and applies a deep convolutional neural network to learn complex features from DNA sequencing data to discover CNVs. We demonstrate that CNV-Net can detect CNVs breakpoints from DNA sequencing data with significantly higher accuracy and efficiency than existing tools.

To the best of our knowledge, CNV-Net is the first tool to use a CNN to detect CNVs from DNA sequencing data and achieve high performance on the standard benchmarks. It changes the problem of CNV detection from a process of statistical modeling requiring professional expertise into a process of optimizing a general deep learning model. Besides, although we benchmark CNV-Net using only human samples in this study, it can also be applied to DNA sequencing data of other species.

One limitation of CNV-Net is its need to know candidate CNV positions, potential start and end positions of CNVs, before applying the CNN. This is because CNV-Net makes predictions on pileup images generated at the start and end positions of candidate CNVs. In our analysis, we used candidate CNV positions in human genomes, which were previously identified by the Genome in a Bottle Consortium using a suite of other algorithms. In genome sequencing datasets where CNVs have not been analyzed, these candidate CNV positions may not be available. One solution to this problem may be using candidate CNV positions detected by other CNV detection algorithms, especially algorithms with high recall, as CNV-Net can remove false positive CNVs from those CNV positions. Additionally, we may also develop a sensitive candidate CNV detection algorithm, which may have low precision but high recall. Our future research direction involves identifying candidate CNV positions from genome sequencing datasets and incorporating them with CNV-Net; nevertheless, CNV-Net demonstrates potential as a valuable and effective tool for CNV detection.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper presents a promising application of deep learning for the challenging task of structural variant calling, specifically framed as an image classification problem. The introduction effectively motivates the necessity of CNV-Net by highlighting the limitations of statistical methods in capturing complex features and the potential for deep learning to address computational bottlenecks. However, a significant concern regarding the methodology is the arbitrary encoding of biological data into RGB pixels; for example, mapping base quality or strand directionality to pixel intensities (e.g., 70 or 240) risks introducing non-linear feature representations that might mislead a CNN, as these values lack the spatial invariance and spectral hierarchy that images typically possess. Furthermore, the reliance on a fixed 201-base window size and the potential loss of information from discarding reads when coverage exceeds 45 raise questions about the model's robustness and its ability to generalize to high-coverage or high-complexity regions of the genome. To improve the quality of the submission, the authors should provide a more rigorous justification for their feature engineering choices and address how the model handles long-range dependencies, given that the input window size is relatively small compared to the wide range of CNV lengths the method intends to detect.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Reference Channel Encoding Logic
comments.quote: The R channel encodes for the nucleotide, which is the same as the reference genome
comments.explanation: The text states the R channel for the 45 read rows encodes the nucleotide using the same mapping as the reference genome. A nucleotide is a categorical property; it is unclear how mismatches (SNVs) are represented in this channel, or if the R channel simply repeats the reference base regardless of the actual read sequence. If it ignores mismatches, the model may be missing critical information for distinguishing sequencing artifacts from true CNVs.

comments.title: Underspecified Training Stopping Criterion
comments.quote: To reduce overfitting, we train the model until the accuracy cannot be improved in 15 epochs.
comments.explanation: The authors refer to 'accuracy' without specifying if this is the categorical accuracy on the training set or the validation set. Monitoring training metrics to address overfitting is technically flawed, as training performance can continue to improve while the model generalizes poorly. Standard practice requires using validation data.

comments.title: Methodological Flaws in Performance and Runtime Comparison
comments.quote: CNV-Net achieves the highest F1-score in CNV detection... CNV-Net is significantly faster than other methods.
comments.explanation: The benchmarking is misleading because CNV-Net is a classifier operating on pre-identified candidate regions, while the comparison methods perform both discovery and genotyping. This 'apples-to-oranges' comparison inflates performance metrics and renders the runtime comparison (16.62s) conceptually invalid, as it excludes the necessary upstream computation time. Additionally, the reported precision in Table 2 is factually inconsistent with the provided data.

comments.title: Lack of Reproducibility regarding Candidate Identification
comments.quote: we assume that candidate CNV positions are given as input to CNV-Net.
comments.explanation: The paper fails to specify how the 'candidate CNV positions' in the test set were generated or what selection criteria were used. Since the model relies entirely on these positions, this is a critical missing link for reproducibility. Furthermore, the reliance on other noisy, high-recall tools to generate these candidates is not adequately justified, as there is no proof that CNV-Net can effectively filter the high volume of false-positive candidates without losing true sensitivity.

Furthermore, the reliance on a fixed 201-base window size and the potential loss of information from discarding reads when coverage exceeds 45 raise questions about the model's robustness and its ability to generalize to high-coverage or high-complexity regions of the genome. To improve the quality of the submission, the authors should provide a more rigorous justification for their feature engineering choices and address how the model handles long-range dependencies, given that the input window size is relatively small compared to the wide range of CNV lengths the method intends to detect.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Reference Channel Encoding Logic
comments.quote: The R channel encodes for the nucleotide, which is the same as the reference genome
comments.explanation: The text states the R channel for the 45 read rows encodes the nucleotide using the same mapping as the reference genome (A: 250, G: 180, T: 100, C: 30). While this describes a single value, a nucleotide is a categorical property. It is unclear how mismatches (SNVs) are represented in this channel, or if the R channel simply repeats the reference base regardless of the actual read sequence. If it ignores mismatches, the model may be missing critical information for distinguishing sequencing artifacts from true CNVs.

comments.title: Contradiction in Training Data Split Size
comments.quote: From the NA12878 dataset, a total of 52,243 pileup images are labeled... split into 70%, 20%, 10%... In the NA12878 image dataset (Pendleton et al., 2015), we generate images for 22,936 duplications, 18,614 deletions, and 10,693 false positives
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2, the authors state there are 52,243 total images. However, in Section 3, they list 22,936 (dups), 18,614 (dels), and 10,693 (FPs), which sum to 52,243. This is consistent. However, the subsequent description of splitting these into training/validation/test sets remains slightly ambiguous regarding whether these splits are maintained across the specific labels or pooled. While likely just a slight oversight in reporting, the numerical consistency is perfect, making this a minor clerical issue rather than a mathematical one.

comments.title: Underspecified Training Stopping Criterion
comments.quote: To reduce overfitting, we train the model until the accuracy cannot be improved in 15 epochs.
comments.explanation: The authors refer to 'accuracy' without specifying if this is the categorical accuracy on the training set or the validation set. Standard deep learning practice requires monitoring validation loss or validation accuracy to prevent overfitting. Monitoring training accuracy (or training loss) to decide when to stop training for 'overfitting' is technically flawed, as training metrics can continue to improve while the model generalizes poorly.

comments.title: Potential Overclaim on Model Comparison
comments.quote: CNV-Net achieves the highest F1-score in CNV detection in the HG002 dataset among all six methods.
comments.explanation: The authors compare CNV-Net (a classifier that assumes candidate breakpoints are provided as input) against methods that perform both discovery and genotyping (the other five tools). By providing CNV-Net with the correct candidate locations (effectively giving it information the other tools must compute themselves), the comparison is not 'apples-to-apples' in terms of problem difficulty. Claiming it is 'more accurate' based on this specific benchmark setup without clearly qualifying the advantage given to their model is potentially misleading.

comments.title: Inconsistent performance metrics in comparison
comments.quote: It also has the highest recall and the second highest precision.
comments.explanation: Looking at Table 2, the authors claim CNV-Net has the 'highest recall' (0.6596) and 'second highest precision' (0.7960). While the recall claim is correct, the precision claim is factually incorrect based on the provided table: Manta has a precision of 0.9282 and Lumpy has a precision of 0.7228. CNV-Net (0.7960) has better precision than Lumpy, but it is not the second highest; it is lower than Manta (0.9282). This misrepresents the comparison data provided.

comments.title: Ambiguity in runtime comparison methodology
comments.quote: CNV-Net is significantly faster than other methods (Table 3). ... Another reason for this can be that CNV-Net makes predictions on candidate CNVs so it does not need to spend time searching for candidate CNVs in DNA sequencing data as if other methods do.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that their runtime is 'front-loaded' by requiring pre-identified candidates, whereas the other tools perform the search themselves. However, the comparison in Table 3 is conceptually flawed because it compares the 'inference' time of a neural network against the 'full pipeline' time (including candidate discovery) of the other tools. This makes the runtime '16.62 seconds' misleading without explicitly stating the time required to generate the candidate positions that the tool relies on. The claim of being 'significantly faster' is technically true for inference, but potentially misleading regarding the total computational cost of the experiment.

comments.title: Underspecified handling of candidate positions
comments.quote: we assume that candidate CNV positions are given as input to CNV-Net. However, other methods find the candidate CNV positions using the information on how reads are mapped to the reference genome
comments.explanation: The authors state that future research involves identifying candidate CNV positions. However, for a user trying to reproduce these results, the paper lacks detail on how the 'candidate CNV positions' in the test set (HG002) were obtained or formatted such that the model could predict them. Since the model relies entirely on these pre-filtered positions, knowing the selection criteria for these 'candidates' is vital; if the candidates are too narrow or require a specific sensitivity, the tool's utility is limited. The paper does not specify if these candidates were derived from the same truth sets used for evaluation or a separate discovery pipeline, which is a major missing link for reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'High Recall' Strategy
comments.quote: One solution to this problem may be using candidate CNV positions detected by other CNV detection algorithms, especially algorithms with high recall, as CNV-Net can remove false positive CNVs from those CNV positions.
comments.explanation: The authors propose using high-recall algorithms to generate candidate positions for CNV-Net. However, high-recall algorithms generally operate by lowering the threshold for detection, which inherently increases the number of false-positive candidates. The authors claim CNV-Net can 'remove' these false positives; however, they have not established that CNV-Net has a sufficiently high precision to filter these out without also discarding a significant portion of true positives, especially since CNV-Net itself exhibited a precision of only 0.6046 for false positives (effectively, a 60% precision in correctly identifying non-CNVs) in their own test data (Table 1). The proposal assumes the CNN can act as an adequate filter on noise, but the moderate precision observed suggests this could severely diminish the overall pipeline performance.

comments.title: Unsupported Claim of 'Significantly' Faster Runtime
comments.quote: CNV-Net is significantly faster than other methods (Table 3). That is because a fitted convolutional neural network is efficient in making predictions, however, it may be time-consuming during model training.
comments.explanation: The authors state that CNV-Net is 'significantly faster' than other methods based on the 16.62 seconds reported in Table 3. However, this runtime only accounts for the inference step on already identified candidate regions. As the authors admit in the conclusion, the need to identify these candidates (using external tools) is a major limitation. If the time required for those external algorithms is included in a fair comparison, the 'significant' speed advantage may disappear. Asserting total performance superiority while excluding the primary 'candidate search' phase of their own stated workflow is misleading to the reader regarding the actual operational efficiency of the system.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper addresses an important and timely problem—detection of copy number variants (CNVs) from short-read whole-genome sequencing—and proposes a convolutional neural network (CNN) approach with an interesting encoding of sequencing data into RGB images. The exposition is generally clear, though the abstract is cut off mid-sentence, which undermines professionalism. The most significant issues are methodological and evaluative. First, the fixed 201×55 pixel image representation, which discards reads beyond 45 per region and uses a rigid 201-base window, may lose critical information for large or high-coverage CNVs, and the rationale for these arbitrary thresholds is not justified. Second, the CNN architecture is extremely shallow (six layers) for a modern deep learning model; it is unclear whether such a small network can learn the complex, multi-scale features needed to distinguish true CNVs from artifacts across diverse genomic contexts. Third, the paper lacks a clear description of how breakpoints are predicted from image classifications (e.g., whether overlapping windows are aggregated, how start/end pairs are resolved) and provides no results, validation strategies (e.g., cross-validation), or rigorous comparison against state-of-the-art methods like DELLY or Manta. Without these, the claimed advantages in accuracy and efficiency remain unsubstantiated. Overall, while the idea is promising, the paper in its current form is incomplete and does not yet meet the standards for a top-tier conference, particularly regarding experimental rigor and architectural justification.

comments.title: Unfair performance comparison with other tools
comments.quote: It is important to note that CNV-Net makes predictions using the pileup images of CNVs where their start and end positions are given, and hence we assume that candidate CNV positions are given as input to CNV-Net. However, other methods find the candidate CNV positions using the information on how reads are mapped to the reference genome and detect CNVs on those candidate CNV positions.
comments.explanation: The authors acknowledge that CNV-Net is evaluated on pre-specified candidate breakpoints (from the benchmark set), while other tools must both discover and classify CNVs. Yet they still present precision, recall, F1, and runtime comparisons (Tables 2 and 3) as if the tasks are equivalent. This is misleading because CNV-Net's input already includes the correct breakpoints, giving it an unfair advantage in both accuracy and speed. Additionally, the runtime comparison in Table 3 compares only CNV-Net's inference time (16.62 seconds) against the full runtime of other tools, ignoring the training phase and the computational effort required to generate candidate CNV positions (which in this study came from prior analysis). The claim that CNV-Net is 'more accurate' and 'faster' is not supported by a fair head-to-head comparison; an appropriate comparison would involve using CNV-Net in a pipeline with a separate candidate caller, or comparing only its classification performance on the same candidate set used by other tools.

comments.title: Underspecified image encoding parameters
comments.quote: The R and G channels are encoded with an adjusted read depth value between 0 and 255. Coverage rows are encoded in RG channels by the number of mapped reads covering each position.
comments.explanation: The method does not specify what 'adjusted' means for mapping read depth (e.g., integer counts) to the 0–255 range (linear scaling, clipping, log transformation?). Similarly, base quality scores (typically Phred 0–40) are said to be 'adjusted' without describing the mapping. Read selection when coverage exceeds 45 reads is also vague ('those reads are discarded' – which ones? Randomly? By alignment order?). Furthermore, the text states that coverage is encoded in both R and G channels with the same adjusted read depth value, which is redundant and ambiguous. It is unclear whether the authors intended to use the two channels to represent forward/reverse strand coverage or to extend the dynamic range. This inconsistency and lack of detail hinder reproducibility.

comments.title: Incomplete and inconsistent network architecture description
comments.quote: CNV-Net consists of four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers with a total of 5,110,979 parameters. CNV-Net employs a six-layer convolutional neural network for detecting copy number variations from DNA sequencing data. Each convolutional layer is followed by batch normalization, average pooling, and 0.4 dropout.
comments.explanation: The network architecture description has multiple issues: (1) The total parameter count of 5,110,979 is given without specifying the number of filters per convolutional layer or the number of nodes in the fully connected layers, making the count unverifiable. (2) The paper calls CNV-Net a 'six-layer convolutional neural network' despite having only four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers; the latter are not convolutional layers, so this is a misleading description. (3) The order of operations after each convolutional layer (batch normalization, average pooling, dropout) is ambiguous; for example, it is unclear whether dropout is applied before or after pooling, and where the ReLU activation fits in the sequence. These ambiguities affect reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistency in total number of training images stated
comments.quote: From the NA12878 dataset, a total of 52,243 pileup images are labeled as duplications, deletions, and false positives from the training set... From the NA12878 image dataset (Pendleton et al., 2015), we generate images for 22,936 duplications, 18,614 deletions, and 10,693 false positives (not CNVs) breakpoints.
comments.explanation: The two quoted sentences provide inconsistent terminology: the first refers to 'pileup images labeled as duplications, deletions, and false positives', while the second refers to images for 'breakpoints'. Since each CNV has two breakpoints (start and end), the count of 52,243 breakpoint images would correspond to roughly half that number of CNVs, but the paper does not clarify whether these are per-CNV or per-breakpoint counts. This inconsistency could mislead readers about the actual number of training examples and the structure of the dataset.

comments.title: Unclear selection of false positive training images
comments.quote: False positive images include random 201-base regions outside of CNVs.
comments.explanation: The method for generating false positive training images is underspecified. The phrase 'random 201-base regions outside of CNVs' does not specify how 'outside of CNVs' is defined (e.g., anywhere in the genome not overlapping any known CNV? Near breakpoints to simulate realistic false positives?). The term 'random' is vague (what distribution?). Additionally, the class imbalance in the training data (22,936 duplications vs 18,614 deletions vs 10,693 false positives) is not discussed; the network may be biased toward duplications. This lack of detail hinders reproducibility.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in 'maximal' instead of 'maximum'
comments.quote: We use a cyclical learning rate initialized at 1e-5 and maximal 1e-2.
comments.explanation: The word 'maximal' is likely a typo for 'maximum' (the maximum learning rate in the cyclical schedule). This is a minor wording issue; the intended meaning is clear, but it should be corrected for clarity.

comments.title: Underspecified image encoding parameters
comments.quote: The R and G channels are encoded with an adjusted read depth value between 0 and 255.
comments.explanation: The method does not specify what 'adjusted' means. How is the raw read depth (e.g., integer counts up to 50× or higher) mapped to the 0–255 range? Is it linear scaling, clipping, or log transformation? Similarly, base quality scores (typically Phred scores 0–40) are said to be 'adjusted to be between 0 and 255' without describing the mapping. Without these details, a reader cannot reproduce the image generation exactly, which is a reproducibility concern. The description of read selection when coverage exceeds 45 reads is also vague ('those reads are discarded' – which ones? Randomly? By alignment order?).

comments.title: Ambiguous use of two channels for a single coverage value
comments.quote: Coverage rows are encoded in RG channels by the number of mapped reads covering each position. The R and G channels are encoded with an adjusted read depth value between 0 and 255.
comments.explanation: The text states that coverage is encoded 'in RG channels' and that both R and G are encoded with the same adjusted read depth value. This suggests that the same scalar is written to both the red and green channels, which is redundant. It is unclear why two channels are used – perhaps a typo or the authors intended to convey something else (e.g., R for forward-strand coverage, G for reverse-strand coverage). The caption of Figure 1 (not fully shown) says 'Coverage rows are encoded in RG channels by the number of mapped reads covering each position.' This could be interpreted as using the two channels jointly to represent a wider range, but the prose in the main text says 'the R and G channels are encoded with an adjusted read depth value' – singular. This inconsistency could confuse readers trying to implement the method.

comments.title: Inconsistent count of total network parameters
comments.quote: CNV-Net consists of four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers with a total of 5,110,979 parameters.
comments.explanation: The text claims a total of 5,110,979 parameters for a network with four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers, given input dimensions of 201×55×3. The description is vague about filter counts per layer (only kernel size 3×3 is given for all convolutional layers). Without specifying the number of filters per layer and the number of nodes in the fully connected layers, it is impossible for a reader to verify this parameter count. The number seems unusually precise and large given the input size; typical networks with such architecture would have far fewer parameters unless the fully connected layers are very large. This lack of specification makes the architecture description underspecified and the parameter count unverifiable.

comments.title: Ambiguity in batch normalization and dropout ordering relative to pooling
comments.quote: Each convolutional layer is followed by batch normalization, average pooling, and 0.4 dropout.
comments.explanation: The order of operations after each convolutional layer is ambiguous. It is unclear whether the sequence is: convolution → batch normalization → average pooling → dropout, or some other ordering. Typical practice varies: batch normalization often comes before activation, and dropout is commonly applied after pooling. The authors list 'batch normalization, average pooling, and 0.4 dropout' without clarifying if dropout is applied before or after pooling, or if activation (ReLU) occurs within this sequence. This ambiguity could affect reproducibility. The ReLU activation is stated earlier ('the RELU activation function') but its position in the pipeline is not specified relative to batch normalization.

comments.title: Contradiction in the number of convolutional vs. total layers
comments.quote: CNV-Net employs a six-layer convolutional neural network for detecting copy number variations from DNA sequencing data.
comments.explanation: The paper calls CNV-Net a 'six-layer deep convolutional neural network' in multiple places (abstract, introduction, section 2.2 caption), but the description in section 2.2 states it consists of 'four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers'. A fully connected layer is not a convolutional layer. Calling a network with only four convolutional layers a 'six-layer convolutional neural network' is misleading; it would be more accurate to describe it as a hybrid CNN with four convolutional and two dense layers. This is a minor framing inconsistency that could confuse readers about the architecture.

comments.title: Questionable comparison of runtime: front-loaded training time ignored
comments.quote: Another reason for this can be that CNV-Net makes predictions on candidate CNVs so it does not need to spend time searching for candidate CNVs in DNA sequencing data as if other methods do.
comments.explanation: The runtime comparison in Table 3 is misleading. The authors compare only the inference time of CNV-Net (16.62 seconds) against the full runtime of other tools, but CNV-Net requires a potentially long training phase (not reported) and, crucially, requires candidate CNV positions as input (which other tools must discover themselves). The paper acknowledges in the Conclusion that 'One limitation of CNV-Net is its need to know candidate CNV positions', and in Section 3 states 'we assume that candidate CNV positions are given as input to CNV-Net'. The runtime comparison is therefore not fair: the candidate positions used in this study came from the Genome in a Bottle Consortium's prior analysis, which itself required computational effort not accounted for. A fair comparison would include the time needed to generate candidate CNV positions, which is a nontrivial step. The claim of superior efficiency is thus overstated.

comments.title: Inconsistency in total number of training images stated
comments.quote: From the NA12878 dataset, a total of 52,243 pileup images are labeled as duplications, deletions, and false positives from the training set... From the NA12878 image dataset (Pendleton et al., 2015), we generate images for 22,936 duplications, 18,614 deletions, and 10,693 false positives (not CNVs) breakpoints.
comments.explanation: The two quoted sentences provide inconsistent counts. The first says 52,243 total images. The second gives three class counts: 22,936 + 18,614 + 10,693 = 52,243, which matches. However, the second sentence says these are images for 'breakpoints' (singular breakpoints per image), and earlier it was stated that each CNV has two breakpoints (start and end). This implies the 22,936 duplications refer to duplication breakpoints, not duplication CNVs. But the first sentence refers to 'pileup images labeled as duplications, deletions, and false positives' — it is ambiguous whether these are per-CNV or per-breakpoint counts. The total of 52,243 breakpoint images would correspond to roughly half that number of CNVs (since each CNV contributes two breakpoints), but the paper does not clarify how many actual CNVs this represents. The text switches between 'CNV' and 'breakpoint' terminology without consistency, which could mislead readers.

comments.title: Unclear selection of false positive training images
comments.quote: False positive images include random 201-base regions outside of CNVs.
comments.explanation: The method for generating false positive training images is underspecified. The paper states 'random 201-base regions outside of CNVs', but does not specify how 'outside of CNVs' is defined: are these regions anywhere in the genome that does not overlap any known CNV? Are they specifically chosen to be near CNV breakpoints to simulate realistic false positives? The phrase 'random' is vague — what distribution is used? Without this detail, the training set construction is not reproducible. Additionally, the class imbalance in the training data (22,936 duplications vs 18,614 deletions vs 10,693 false positives) is not discussed; the network might be biased toward duplications given the larger sample size.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in 'maximal' instead of 'maximum'
comments.quote: We use a cyclical learning rate initialized at 1e-5 and maximal 1e-2.
comments.explanation: The word 'maximal' is likely an OCR error or author's typo for 'maximum' (the maximum learning rate in the cyclical schedule). This is a minor wording issue that does not affect understanding, as the intended meaning is clear.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript presents a clear and well-motivated introduction to CNV detection, effectively articulating the limitations of existing statistical callers and proposing a conceptually novel pipeline that translates sequencing metadata into image-based inputs for convolutional neural networks. The writing is generally accessible, and the pileup image construction is described with sufficient structural detail to understand the intended input representation. However, several critical issues undermine the paper’s current rigor and persuasiveness. First, empirical validation is entirely absent in this excerpt: no accuracy metrics, cross-dataset generalization results, or runtime comparisons are provided, which directly challenges the title’s “accurate and fast” claims and overlooks the typical inference latency and memory footprint of CNN-based pipelines. Second, the RGB encoding scheme relies on somewhat arbitrary, fixed-value mappings (e.g., nucleotides to single integers, adjusted coverage/quality ranges), which risks information compression, feature saturation, and reduced gradient signal for the network. Third, essential methodological components are missing or underspecified, including the loss function, class-imbalance handling, breakpoint refinement strategy, training protocol, and baseline comparisons against contemporary, widely adopted CNV callers. For ICLR standards, the authors must substantiate performance with rigorous quantitative benchmarks, provide ablation studies justifying architectural and encoding choices, and explicitly address computational efficiency relative to existing tools.

comments.title: Ambiguity in read nucleotide encoding scheme
comments.quote: The R channel encodes for the nucleotide, which is the same as the reference genome, and the G channel encodes for the base quality found in the BAM file, which is adjusted to be between 0 and 255.
comments.explanation: The authors do an excellent job of explicitly mapping reference bases to numeric R-channel values (A: 250, G: 180, etc.) in the reference rows. However, when describing the read rows, the phrase 'which is the same as the reference genome' is initially confusing. I first wondered if the authors meant that the numeric encoding scheme is identical for reads, or if they meant the read base itself is forcibly overwritten by the reference base. In standard pileup representations, preserving the actual read bases (including mismatches and sequencing errors) is critical for downstream models to detect variants. I checked the surrounding text and the figure caption, which mentions reads are encoded by 'base types,' suggesting the encoding values are reused for actual read bases. However, the phrasing remains ambiguous and could mislead a reproducer into implementing a reference-overwrite strategy, which would strip away variant signals. Clarification on whether the actual read base or the reference base populates the R-channel is necessary.

comments.title: Overstatement on exact data reconstruction capability
comments.quote: Each 201 _×_ 55 pixel pileup image, thus, captures coverage and reads specific to the 201-base region of the reference genome such that the sequencing data may be re-created from a series of consecutive pileup images.
comments.explanation: The authors state that the pileup representation allows the original sequencing data to be re-created, which is a strong claim for an image-based compression scheme. I initially questioned this because image conversions typically lose information. Checking the subsequent sentence resolved part of the concern: the authors note that 'If there are more than 45 reads mapping to a specific region, those reads are discarded.' This truncation policy means that in high-coverage regions, the specific identities, order, and full count of reads beyond the 45th are permanently lost. Consequently, exact reconstruction of the original sequencing data is only possible for low-coverage regions or requires approximations. The claim should be tempered (e.g., 'for low-coverage regions' or 'within captured reads') to accurately reflect the lossy nature of the representation in high-depth areas.

comments.title: Missing preposition in window description
comments.quote: capturing the 100 positions left and right each CNV breakpoint.
comments.explanation: The authors clearly intend to describe a 201-base window centered on the breakpoint, capturing 100 positions on either side. However, the phrase 'left and right each CNV breakpoint' is missing the preposition 'of'. While a careful reader in the field will instantly understand the intended meaning, the grammatical omission interrupts the prose and is a minor editing oversight. It does not affect technical comprehension but should be corrected for polishing.

comments.title: Ambiguous dataset split description
comments.quote: From the NA12878 dataset, a total of 52,243 pileup images are labeled as duplications, deletions, and false positives from the training set randomly split into 70%, 20%, 10% categories for training set, validation set, and test set for the network, respectively.
comments.explanation: I initially found this sentence grammatically confusing. The phrase 'from the training set randomly split' suggests that the 70/20/10 partition happens within an already-defined training subset, which contradicts standard machine learning practice where the entire dataset is partitioned into train/validation/test splits. Reading the sentence again, I believe the authors intended to say that the 52,243 total images are randomly split into the three categories. However, as written, the clause structure creates ambiguity about how the data partitions were actually constructed. Clarifying that the full dataset is split would prevent misinterpretation of the experimental setup.

comments.title: Underspecified early stopping criterion
comments.quote: To reduce overfitting, we train the model until the accuracy cannot be improved in 15 epochs.
comments.explanation: The text states that training stops when accuracy cannot be improved for 15 epochs, which describes an early stopping mechanism. However, it does not specify whether this patience parameter is monitored using training accuracy or validation accuracy. Given the stated goal is to 'reduce overfitting,' the standard convention is to monitor validation performance. Without this detail, a reader cannot reproduce the exact training protocol, as stopping on training accuracy would defeat the purpose of preventing overfitting. I flag this as a minor methodological gap that affects reproducibility.

comments.title: Unreported class-wise performance on HG002 and ambiguous F1 calculation
comments.quote: Next, we evaluate our fitted CNV-Net on the HG002 dataset and compare it with five existing tools for CNV detection. ... As demonstrated in Table 2, CNV-Net achieves the highest F1-score in CNV detection in the HG002 dataset among all six methods.
comments.explanation: On the NA12878 test set, the authors report separate F1-scores for duplications, deletions, and false positives (Table 1). However, on the HG002 dataset, Table 2 reports a single aggregated F1-score for 'CNV detection.' The text notes that the filtered HG002 dataset contains 174 duplications but 10,668 deletions. The authors do not clarify whether duplication and deletion classes are merged for evaluation, or whether a macro/micro-average is used. Given the extreme class imbalance, reporting a single F1-score obscures the model's performance on duplications specifically. This ambiguity is moderate because it affects how readers should interpret the comparative performance claims, especially since duplications are typically harder to detect and the aggregated metric could mask poor sensitivity on that class.

comments.title: Awkward phrasing regarding network flattening and output sequence
comments.quote: Arrays are flattened in between the four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers followed by softmax output to predictions for CNV breakpoint presence in the 201-base window: duplication, deletion, or false positive (not a CNV).
comments.explanation: The description of the network architecture contains a slightly ambiguous structural phrase: 'in between the four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers.' I checked the context to see if the intended meaning was clear, and while the authors likely mean that flattening occurs after the conv blocks and before the FC layers, the phrasing could be misread as occurring during the transition or within the layers themselves. Additionally, the run-on continuation 'followed by softmax output...' makes the exact layer sequence harder to parse. Rewriting this to explicitly state the order (e.g., 'After the four convolutional layers, the tensors are flattened before passing through two fully connected layers and a softmax output...') would improve clarity and reproducibility of the model description.

comments.title: Unreported class-wise performance on HG002 and ambiguous F1 calculation
comments.quote: Next, we evaluate our fitted CNV-Net on the HG002 dataset and compare it with five existing tools for CNV detection. ... As demonstrated in Table 2, CNV-Net achieves the highest F1-score in CNV detection in the HG002 dataset among all six methods.
comments.explanation: On the NA12878 test set, the authors report separate F1-scores for duplications, deletions, and false positives (Table 1). However, on the HG002 dataset, Table 2 reports a single aggregated F1-score for 'CNV detection.' The text notes that the filtered HG002 dataset contains 174 duplications but 10,668 deletions. The authors do not clarify whether duplication and deletion classes are merged for evaluation, or whether a macro/micro-average is used. Given the extreme class imbalance, reporting a single F1-score obscures the model's performance on duplications specifically. This ambiguity is moderate because it affects how readers should interpret the comparative performance claims, especially since duplications are typically harder to detect and the aggregated metric could mask poor sensitivity on that class.

comments.title: Awkward phrasing regarding network flattening and output sequence
comments.quote: Arrays are flattened in between the four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers followed by softmax output to predictions for CNV breakpoint presence in the 201-base window: duplication, deletion, or false positive (not a CNV).
comments.explanation: The description of the network architecture contains a slightly ambiguous structural phrase: 'in between the four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers.' I checked the context to see if the intended meaning was clear, and while the authors likely mean that flattening occurs after the conv blocks and before the FC layers, the phrasing could be misread as occurring during the transition or within the layers themselves. Additionally, the run-on continuation 'followed by softmax output...' makes the exact layer sequence harder to parse. Rewriting this to explicitly state the order (e.g., 'After the four convolutional layers, the tensors are flattened before passing through two fully connected layers and a softmax output...') would improve clarity and reproducibility of the model description.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel application of deep learning to the bioinformatics task of copy number variant (CNV) detection, offering a clear and accessible explanation of the proposed "Image-to-CNN" approach. However, the most significant issue currently is the lack of methodological justification for the specific hyperparameters chosen for the model. The authors describe an arbitrary encoding scheme (e.g., A: 250, G: 180) and a fixed image resolution (201x55 pixels) without explaining the rationale behind these choices or performing necessary ablation studies. Furthermore, while the paper claims high accuracy and fast detection, benchmarking a simple six-layer CNN against complex, established statistical methods (e.g., Rausch et al., Abyzov et al.) that often employ sophisticated feature engineering requires a rigorous demonstration that the CNN can effectively capture the necessary genomic features without deep architectural complexity. The abstract cuts off before presenting these results, so the claimed advantages must be defended with compelling evidence regarding the trade-off between model simplicity and biological discrimination power.

comments.title: Inconsistency between read length and image height
comments.quote: 201 × 55 pixel image... 45 rows capture the mapped reads... each read in the sequencing data is 101 bases long... Reads in the HG002 dataset are all 148 bases long.
comments.explanation: The paper defines the input image as 55 pixels tall, with 45 of these vertical rows dedicated to mapped reads. It explicitly states that NA12878 reads are 101 bases long and HG002 reads are 148 bases long. A 148-base read cannot be visually represented in 45 rows of pixels if the representation is linear (1 pixel per base) or even densely packed diagonally in a way that preserves the alignment relative to the breakpoint, which is crucial for CNV detection. The text implies the model can 'see' the reads through these rows, but the dimensions make this physically impossible.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of read base encoding
comments.quote: Reads are encoded in RGB channels by base types, base quality, and strand directionality... R channel encodes for the nucleotide, which is the same as the reference genome... G channel encodes for base quality... B channel encodes for strand directionality.
comments.explanation: The Figure 1 caption states that RGB channels encode 'base types' for reads. However, the body text describes the encoding as follows: the R channel holds the 'nucleotide' (clarified as the reference genome), the G channel holds 'base quality', and the B channel holds 'strand directionality'. This leaves no channel or description for the *read's actual base* (the allele), which is essential for detecting mismatches (SNVs/indels) that contribute to CNV breakpoints. Without knowing how mismatches are encoded, the method cannot be accurately implemented or understood.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of 'adjusted' read depth or quality
comments.quote: R and G channels are encoded with an adjusted read depth value between 0 and 255... base quality found in the BAM file, which is adjusted to be between 0 and 255.
comments.explanation: The text claims values are 'adjusted' to fit into 0-255, but fails to specify the normalization or scaling method used. Without a fixed formula (e.g., `round(quality * 255 / max_quality)`), the normalization is underspecified, potentially leading to inconsistent behavior between implementations or reducing the utility of the image encoding.

comments.title: Runtime vs. Training Narrative Error
comments.quote: That is because a fitted convolutional neural network is efficient in making predictions, however, it may be time-consuming during model training. Run time is effectively front-loaded into model training instead of a concurrent statistical analysis.
comments.explanation: The paragraph discusses the runtime comparison in Table 3, which lists inference times (CNV-Net: 16.62 seconds) that are drastically lower than the statistical tools (e.g., BreakDancer: 92 minutes). However, the text concludes that this runtime is 'effectively front-loaded into model training.' This is a logical contradiction. Training is an offline phase that occurs once, whereas the runtime in Table 3 refers to the prediction phase (inference) on the HG002 dataset. It is impossible for the inference runtime to be 'front-loaded' into training. The authors appear to be confusing the computational effort required for training with the wall-clock runtime for processing the dataset.

comments.title: Ambiguous Methodology for Comparative Benchmarking
comments.quote: It is important to note that CNVNet makes predictions using the pileup images of CNVs where their start and end positions are given, and hence we assume that candidate CNV positions are given as input to CNV-Net. However, other methods find the candidate CNV positions using the information on how reads are mapped to the reference genome and detect CNVs on those candidate CNV positions.
comments.explanation: The text highlights a fundamental difference in approach: CNV-Net requires pre-specified genomic windows (breakpoints) to generate images, while statistical tools (Manta, Delly, etc.) must scan the genome to identify candidates. While this is noted as a difference, it does not explicitly state how the comparison was conducted experimentally. For instance, did the authors run CNV-Net only on the *ground truth* breakpoints (a small set of images) to report Table 2, while running the statistical tools on the *entire genome* (billions of windows) to report Table 2 and 3? If so, the speed comparison (Table 3) is inherently biased in favor of CNV-Net, as it requires minimal computation compared to a genome-wide scan. The paper acknowledges this limitation in Section 4, but the presentation in Section 3 frames it as a direct performance comparison that obfuscates this methodological asymmetry.

comments.title: Paragraph Narrative Conflict
comments.quote: We train the convolutional neural network on the NA12878 dataset and test the fitted model on the HG002 dataset... We train the CNV-Net with the training set and report its performance metrics on the test set of the NA12878 dataset.
comments.explanation: The first sentence of the paragraph describes an experiment where the model is trained on NA12878 and tested on HG002. Immediately following this, the text describes a train/test split on the NA12878 dataset and claims to report metrics on the NA12878 test set (Table 1). The phrasing is somewhat disjointed; it suggests two distinct evaluations (internal cross-validation and cross-dataset generalization) are happening simultaneously or the narrative flow is jumbled. While the logic is not impossible, the transition between 'testing on HG002' and 'reporting on NA12878' without a clear delineation of which results correspond to which experiment setup creates a logical ambiguity.

comments.title: Unreadable author name due to OCR artifacts
comments.quote: Morten K¨allberg
comments.explanation: While reviewing the reference list, the name 'Morten K¨allberg' contains garbled characters (the umlaut/inverted 'e' symbol '¨') which are clearly artifacts of OCR processing. The context suggests the intended names are 'Morten Källberg' (or similar) from the original sources. This renders the reference list difficult to read, though it does not affect the paper's mathematical or logical arguments.

comments.title: Imprecise terminology: 'general deep learning model'
comments.quote: changes the problem of CNV detection from a process of statistical modeling requiring professional expertise into a process of optimizing a general deep learning model.
comments.explanation: The authors describe CNV-Net (a specific 6-layer CNN architecture) as a 'general deep learning model.' While CNNs are indeed universal function approximators, referring to a specific, fixed-architecture model as 'general' is technically imprecise and slightly misleading. A more accurate description would be 'optimizing a specific CNN architecture' or 'making inferences using a deep neural network.' This is a stylistic/logical imprecision that could confuse a reader regarding the specificity of the tool.

comments.title: Questionable novelty claim: 'first tool to use a CNN'
comments.quote: To the best of our knowledge, CNV-Net is the first tool to use a CNN to detect CNVs from DNA sequencing data and achieve high performance on the standard benchmarks.
comments.explanation: As a reviewer aware of the state of the field in 2026, it is highly probable that this claim is factually incorrect. Prior to this paper, works such as 'DeepCNV' (Xie et al., 2018) utilized convolutional neural networks for copy number variation inference from whole-genome sequencing data. The claim undermines the paper's novelty and relies on a disclaimer ('To the best of our knowledge') that does not hold up against existing literature.
